segments.text: "I swear, if the aliens end up eating the lot of us, we'll have the Church of Game Theory to thank for it," Sascha said.

runs.t: "I swear, if the aliens end up eating the lot of us, we'll have the Church of Game Theory to thank for it," Sascha said.

segments.text: She was grabbing a brick of couscous from the galley. I was there for the caffeine. We were more or less alone; the rest of the crew was strewn from dome to Fab.

runs.t: She was grabbing a brick of couscous from the galley. I was there for the caffeine. We were more or less alone; the rest of the crew was strewn from dome to Fab.

segments.text: "Linguists don't use it?" I knew some that did.

runs.t: "Linguists don't use it?" I knew some that did.

segments.text: "We don't." And the others are hacks. "Thing about game theory is, it assumes rational self-interest among the players. And people just aren't rational."

runs.t: . "Thing about game theory is, it assumes rational self-interest among the players. And people just aren't

segments.text: "It used to assume that," I allowed. "These days they factor in the social neurology."

runs.t: "It used to assume that," I allowed. "These days they factor in the social neurology."

segments.text: "Human social neurology." She bit a corner off her brick, spoke around a mouthful of semolina. "That's what game theory's good for. Rational players, or human ones. And let me take a wild stab here and wonder if either of those is gonna apply to that." She waved her hand at some archetypal alien lurking past the bulkhead.

runs.t: social neurology." She bit a corner off her brick, spoke around a mouthful of semolina. "That's what game theory's good for. Rational players, or human ones. And let me take a wild stab here and wonder if either of those is gonna apply to
." She waved her hand at some archetypal alien lurking past the bulkhead.

segments.text: "It's got its limitations," I admitted. "I guess you use the tools you can lay your hands on."

runs.t: "It's got its limitations," I admitted. "I guess you use the tools you can lay your hands on."

segments.text: Sascha snorted. "So if you couldn't get your hands on a proper set of blueprints, you'd base your dream home on a book of dirty limericks."

runs.t: Sascha snorted. "So if you couldn't get your hands on a proper set of blueprints, you'd base your dream home on a book of dirty limericks."

segments.text: "Maybe not." And then, a bit defensive in spite of myself, I added, "I've found it useful, though. In areas you might not expect it to be."

runs.t: "Maybe not." And then, a bit defensive in spite of myself, I added, "I've found it useful, though. In areas you might not expect it to be."

segments.text: "Yeah? Name one."
"Birthdays," I said, and immediately wished I hadn't.

runs.t: "Birthdays," I said, and immediately wished I hadn't.

segments.text: Sascha stopped chewing. Something behind her eyes flickered, almost strobed, as if her other selves were pricking up their ears.

runs.t: Sascha stopped chewing. Something behind her eyes flickered, almost
, as if her other selves were pricking up their ears.

segments.text: "Go on," she said, and I could feel the whole Gang listening in.

runs.t: "Go on," she said, and I could feel the whole Gang listening in.

segments.text: "It's nothing, really. Just an example."
"So. Tell us." Sascha cocked James' head at me.

runs.t: "So. Tell us." Sascha cocked James' head at me.

segments.text: I shrugged. No point making a big thing out of it. "Well, according to game theory, you should never tell anyone when your birthday is."

runs.t: I shrugged. No point making a big thing out of it. "Well, according to game theory, you should never tell anyone when your birthday is."

segments.text: "I don't follow."
"It's a lose-lose proposition. There's no winning strategy."

runs.t: "It's a lose-lose proposition. There's no winning strategy."

segments.text: "What do you mean, strategy? It's a birthday."

runs.t: "What do you mean, strategy? It's a

segments.text: Chelsea had said exactly the same thing when I'd tried to explain it to her. Look, I'd said, say you tell everyone when it is and nothing happens. It's kind of a slap in the face.

runs.t: Chelsea had said exactly the same thing when I'd tried to explain it to her.
say you tell everyone when it is and nothing happens. It's kind of a slap in the face.

segments.text: Or suppose they throw you a party, Chelsea had replied.

runs.t: Or suppose they throw you a party

segments.text: Then you don't know whether they're doing it sincerely, or if your earlier interaction just guilted them into observing an occasion they'd rather have ignored. But if you don't tell anyone, and nobody commemorates the event, there's no reason to feel badly because after all, nobody knew. And if someone does buy you a drink then you know it's sincere because nobody would go to all the trouble of finding out when your birthday is— and then celebrating it—if they didn't honestly like you.

segments.text: Of course, the Gang was more up to speed on such things. I didn't have to explain it verbally: I could just grab a piece of ConSensus and plot out the payoff matrix, Tell/Don't Tell along the columns, Celebrated/Not Celebrated along the rows, the unassailable black-and-white logic of cost and benefit in the squares themselves. The math was irrefutable: the one winning strategy was concealment. Only fools revealed their birthdays.

along the rows, the unassailable black-and-white logic of cost and benefit in the squares themselves. The math was irrefutable: the one winning strategy was concealment. Only fools revealed their birthdays.

segments.text: Sascha looked at me. "You ever show this to anyone else?"

runs.t: Sascha looked at me. "You ever show this to anyone else?"

segments.text: "Sure. My girlfriend."
Her eyebrows lifted. "You had a girlfriend? A real one?"

runs.t: had a girlfriend? A real one?"

segments.text: I nodded. "Once."
"I mean after you showed this to her."
"Well, yes."
"Uh huh." Her eyes wandered back to the payoff matrix. "Just curious, Siri. How did she react?"

runs.t: "Uh huh." Her eyes wandered back to the payoff matrix. "Just curious, Siri. How did she react?"

segments.text: "She didn't, really. Not at first. Then—well, she laughed."

runs.t: "She didn't, really. Not at first. Then—well, she laughed."

segments.text: "Better woman than me." Sascha shook her head. "I'd have dumped you on the spot."

runs.t: "Better woman than me." Sascha shook her head. "I'd have dumped you on the spot."

segments.text: My nightly constitutional up the spine: glorious dreamy flight along a single degree of freedom. I sailed through hatches and corridors, threw my arms wide and spun in the gentle cyclonic breezes of the drum. Bates ran circles around me, bouncing her ball against bins and bulkheads, stretching to field each curving rebound in the torqued pseudograv. The toy ricocheted off a stairwell and out of reach as I passed; the major's curses followed me through the needle's eye from crypt to bridge.

runs.t: My nightly constitutional up the spine: glorious dreamy flight along a single degree of freedom. I sailed through hatches and corridors, threw my arms wide and spun in the gentle cyclonic breezes of the drum. Bates ran circles around me, bouncing her ball against bins and bulkheads, stretching to field each curving rebound in the torqued pseudograv. The toy ricocheted off a stairwell and out of reach as I passed; the major's curses followed me through the needle's eye from crypt to bridge.

segments.text: I braked just short of the dome, stopped by the sound of quiet voices from ahead.

runs.t: I braked just short of the dome, stopped by the sound of quiet voices from ahead.

segments.text: "Of course they're beautiful," Szpindel murmured. "They're stars."
"And I'm guessing I'm not your first choice to share the view," James said.

runs.t: "And I'm guessing I'm not your first choice to share the view," James said.

segments.text: "You're a close second. But I've got a date with Meesh."

runs.t: "You're a close second. But I've got a date with Meesh."

segments.text: "She never mentioned it."
"She doesn't tell you everything. Ask her."

runs.t: "She doesn't tell you everything. Ask her."

segments.text: "Hey, this body's taking its antilibs. Even if yours isn't."

runs.t: body's taking its antilibs. Even if yours isn't."

segments.text: "Mind out of the gutter, Suze. Eros is only one kind of love, eh? Ancient Greeks recognized four."

runs.t: "Mind out of the gutter, Suze. Eros is only one kind of love, eh? Ancient Greeks recognized four."

segments.text: "Riiight." Definitely not Susan, not any more. "Figures you'd take your lead from a bunch of sodomites."

runs.t: "Riiight." Definitely not Susan, not any more. "Figures you'd take your lead from a bunch of sodomites."

segments.text: "Fuck, Sascha. All I'm asking is a few minutes alone with Meesh before the whip starts cracking again..."

runs.t: , Sascha. All I'm asking is a few minutes alone with Meesh before the whip starts cracking again..."

segments.text: "My body too, Ike. You wanna pull your eyes over my wool?"

runs.t: "My body too, Ike. You wanna pull your eyes over

segments.text: "I just want to talk, eh? Alone. That too much to ask?"
I heard Sascha take a breath.

runs.t: I heard Sascha take a breath.

segments.text: I heard Michelle let it out.

runs.t: I heard Michelle let it out.

segments.text: "Sorry, kid. You know the Gang."
"Thank God. It's like some group inspection whenever I come looking for face time."

runs.t: . It's like some group inspection whenever I come looking for face time."

segments.text: "I guess you're lucky they like you, then."

runs.t: "I guess you're lucky they like you, then."

segments.text: "I still say you ought to stage a coup."

runs.t: "I still say you ought to stage a coup."

segments.text: "You could always move in with us."

runs.t: "You could always move in with us."

segments.text: I heard the rustle of bodies in gentle contact. "How are you?" Szpindel asked. "You okay?"

runs.t: I heard the rustle of bodies in gentle contact. "How are you?" Szpindel asked. "You okay?"

segments.text: "Pretty good. I think I'm finally used to being alive again. You?"

runs.t: "Pretty good. I think I'm finally used to being alive again. You?"

segments.text: "Hey, I'm a spaz no matter how long I've been dead."

runs.t: "Hey, I'm a spaz no matter how long I've been dead."

segments.text: "You get the job done."
"Why, merci. I try."
A small silence. Theseus hummed quietly to herself.
"Mom was right," Michelle said. "They are beautiful."
"What do you see, when you look at them?" And then, catching himself: "I mean—"

runs.t: "What do you see, when you look at them?" And then, catching himself: "I mean—"

segments.text: "They're—prickly," Michelle told him. "When I turn my head it's like bands of very fine needles rolling across my skin in waves. But it doesn't hurt at all. It just tingles. It's almost electric. It's nice."

runs.t: "They're—prickly," Michelle told him. "When I turn my head it's like bands of very fine needles rolling across my skin in waves. But it doesn't hurt at all. It just tingles. It's almost electric. It's nice."

segments.text: "Wish I could feel it that way."

runs.t: "Wish I could feel it that way."

segments.text: "You've got the interface. Just patch a camera into your parietal lobe instead of your visual cortex."

runs.t: "You've got the interface. Just patch a camera into your parietal lobe instead of your visual cortex."

segments.text: "That'd just tell me how a machine feels vision, eh? Still wouldn't know how you do."

runs.t: feels vision, eh? Still wouldn't know how

segments.text: "Isaac Szpindel. You're a romantic."
"Nah."
"You don't want to know. You want to keep it mysterious."

runs.t: to know. You want to keep it mysterious."

segments.text: "Already got more than enough mystery to deal with out here, in case you hadn't noticed."

runs.t: to deal with out here, in case you hadn't noticed."

segments.text: "Yeah, but we can't do anything about that."
"That'll change. We'll be working our asses off in no time."

runs.t: "That'll change. We'll be working our asses off in no time."

segments.text: "You think?"
"Count on it," Szpindel said. "So far we've just been peeking from a distance, eh? Bet all kinds of interesting stuff happens when we get in there and start poking with a stick."

runs.t: "Count on it," Szpindel said. "So far we've just been peeking from a distance, eh? Bet all kinds of interesting stuff happens when we get in there and start poking with a stick."

segments.text: "Maybe for you. There's got to be a biological somewhere in the mix, with all those organics."

runs.t: "Maybe for you. There's got to be a biological
in the mix, with all those organics."

segments.text: "Damn right. And you'll be talking to 'em while I'm giving them their physicals."

runs.t: "Damn right. And you'll be talking to 'em while I'm giving them their physicals."

segments.text: "Maybe not. I mean, Mom would never admit it in a million years but you had a point about language. When you get right down to it, it's a workaround. Like trying to describe dreams with smoke signals. It's noble, it's maybe the most noble thing a body can do but you can't turn a sunset into a string of grunts without losing something. It's limiting. Maybe whatever's out here doesn't even use it."

. Maybe whatever's out here doesn't even use it."

segments.text: "Bet they do, though."
"Since when? You're the one who's always pointing out how inefficient language is."

segments.text: "Only when I'm trying to get under your skin. Your pants—whole other thing." He laughed at his own joke. "Seriously, what are they gonna to use instead, telepathy? I say you'll be up to your elbows in hieroglyphics before you know it. And what's more, you'll decode 'em in record time."

runs.t: "Only when I'm trying to get under your skin. Your pants—whole other thing." He laughed at his own joke. "Seriously, what are they gonna to use instead, telepathy? I say you'll be up to your elbows in hieroglyphics before you know it. And what's more, you'll decode 'em in record time."

segments.text: "You're sweet, but I wonder. Half the time I can't even decode Jukka." Michelle fell silent a moment. "He actually kind of throws me sometimes."

runs.t: "You're sweet, but I wonder. Half the time I can't even decode
." Michelle fell silent a moment. "He actually kind of throws me sometimes."

segments.text: "You and seven billion others."
"Yeah. I know it's silly, but when he's not around there's a part of me that can't stop wondering where he's hiding. And when he's right there in front of me, I feel like I should be hiding."

segments.text: "Not his fault he creeps us out."

runs.t: "Not his fault he creeps us out."

segments.text: "I know. But it's hardly a big morale booster. What genius came up with the idea of putting a vampire in charge?"

runs.t: "I know. But it's hardly a big morale booster. What genius came up with the idea of putting a vampire in charge?"

segments.text: "Where else you going to put them, eh? You want to be the one giving orders to him?"

runs.t: "Where else you going to put them, eh? You want to be the one giving orders to

segments.text: "And it's not just the way he moves. It's the way he talks. It's just wrong."

runs.t: "And it's not just the way he moves. It's the way he

segments.text: "You know he—"
"I'm not talking about the present-tense thing, or all the glottals. He—well, you know how he talks. He's terse."

runs.t: "I'm not talking about the present-tense thing, or all the glottals. He—well, you know how he talks. He's

segments.text: "It's efficient."

"It's artificial, Isaac. He's smarter than all of us put together, but sometimes he talks like he's got a fifty-word vocabulary." A soft snort. "It's not like it'd kill him to use an adverb once in a while."

runs.t: , Isaac. He's smarter than all of us put together, but sometimes he talks like he's got a fifty-word vocabulary." A soft snort. "It's not like it'd kill him to use an adverb once in a while."

segments.text: "Ah. But you say that because you're a linguist, and you can't see why anyone wouldn't want to wallow in the sheer beauty of language." Szpindel harrumphed with mock pomposity. "Now me, I'm a biologist, so it makes perfect sense."

runs.t: "Ah. But you say that because you're a linguist, and you can't see why anyone wouldn't want to wallow in the sheer beauty of
with mock pomposity. "Now me, I'm a biologist, so it makes perfect sense."

segments.text: "Really. Then explain it to me, oh wise and powerful mutilator of frogs."

runs.t: "Really. Then explain it to me, oh wise and powerful mutilator of frogs."

segments.text: "Simple. Bloodsucker's a transient, not a resident."

runs.t: "Simple. Bloodsucker's a transient, not a resident."

segments.text: "What are—oh, those are killer whales, right? Whistle dialects."

runs.t: "What are—oh, those are killer whales, right? Whistle dialects."

segments.text: "I said forget the language. Think about the lifestyle. Residents are fish-eaters, eh? They hang out in big groups, don't move around much, talk all the time." I heard a whisper of motion, imagined Szpindel leaning in and laying a hand on Michelle's arm. I imagined the sensors in his gloves telling him what she felt like. "Transients, now—they eat mammals. Seals, sea lions, smart prey. Smart enough to take cover when they hear a fluke slap or a click train. So transients are sneaky, eh? Hunt in small groups, range all over the place, keep their mouths shut so nobody hears 'em coming."

prey. Smart enough to take cover when they hear a fluke slap or a click train. So transients are
, eh? Hunt in small groups, range all over the place, keep their mouths shut so nobody hears 'em coming."

segments.text: "And Jukka's a transient."

"Man's instincts tell him to keep quiet around prey. Every time he opens his mouth, every time he lets us see him, he's fighting his own brain stem. Maybe we shouldn't be too harsh on the ol' guy just because he's not the world's best motivational speaker, eh?"

runs.t: "Man's instincts tell him to keep quiet around prey. Every time he opens his mouth, every time he lets us
him, he's fighting his own brain stem. Maybe we shouldn't be too harsh on the ol' guy just because he's not the world's best motivational speaker, eh?"

segments.text: "He's fighting the urge to eat us every time we have a briefing? That's reassuring."

runs.t: "He's fighting the urge to eat us every time we have a briefing? That's reassuring."

segments.text: Szpindel chuckled. "It's probably not that bad. I guess even killer whales let their guard down after making a kill. Why sneak around on a full stomach, eh?"

runs.t: Szpindel chuckled. "It's probably not that bad. I guess even killer whales let their guard down after making a kill. Why sneak around on a full stomach, eh?"

segments.text: "So he's not fighting his brain stem. He just isn't hungry."

runs.t: fighting his brain stem. He just isn't hungry."

segments.text: "Probably a little of both. Brain stem never really goes away, you know. But I'll tell you one thing." Some of the playfulness ebbed from Szpindel's voice. "I've got no problem if Sarasti wants to run the occasional briefing from his quarters. But the moment we stop seeing him altogether? That's when you start watching your back."

runs.t: "Probably a little of both. Brain stem never really

, you know. But I'll tell you one thing." Some of the playfulness ebbed from Szpindel's voice. "I've got no problem if Sarasti wants to run the occasional briefing from his quarters. But the moment we stop seeing him altogether? That's when you start watching your back."
